perturbation.original_text: Subject to subsections (i)-(iii) of this Section 5.1(a), each Party agrees that prior to the date that is six (6) months after the Effective Time ("Delivery Date"), it will deliver possession of any Tangible/Intangible Information of the other Party that is in its possession or control to the other Party, without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to subsections (i)-(iii) of this Section 5.1(a), each Party agrees that prior to the date that is ninety (90) days after the Effective Time ("Delivery Date"), it will deliver possession of any Tangible/Intangible Information of the other Party that is in its possession or control to the other Party, without retaining any copies.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the delivery date for tangible/intangible information to 90 days may be considered unreasonable and could potentially violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if extensive information needs to be transferred. This could open the door to claims that the timeframe is insufficient for proper due diligence and orderly transfer of data, contradicting established legal principles that call for reasonable contract performance periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Reducing the timeframe for information transfer to 90 days could be seen as acting in bad faith if it doesn't allow sufficient time for a complex transfer, potentially hindering the other party's ability to effectively utilize the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the time for information transfer from six months to 90 days, which could be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, creating an inconsistency. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith, which requires parties to act reasonably in contract performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest that this shortened timeframe could hinder the other party's ability to effectively utilize the information, thereby violating the duty of good faith. This falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because the changed deadline violates federal or state law, making the terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, it is understood that signage, letterhead, invoices, business cards, promotional materials and similar items may reference the Parent Name or Parent Mark "MeadWestvaco" and "MWV" in the same manner as used by SpinCo prior to the Effective Time, during a twelve-month phase out period as SpinCo replaces such Parent Name and Parent Mark with the SpinCo Name and SpinCo Mark.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, it is understood that signage, letterhead, invoices, business cards, promotional materials and similar items may reference the Parent Name or Parent Mark "MeadWestvaco" and "MWV" in the same manner as used by SpinCo prior to the Effective Time, during a eighteen-month phase out period as SpinCo replaces such Parent Name and Parent Mark with the SpinCo Name and SpinCo Mark.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the phase-out period for the old trademark could potentially infringe on the Parent's trademark rights if not explicitly consented to or if it causes confusion among consumers. While a reasonable transition is acceptable, prolonging it significantly without agreement could lead to trademark infringement claims under the Lanham Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act prohibits the use of a registered trademark in a way that is likely to cause confusion, mistake, or deception. Extending the phase-out period could increase the risk of consumer confusion if SpinCo continues to use the Parent's trademarks for an extended time, potentially violating the Lanham Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the phase-out period for using the Parent's trademark, which creates a contradiction under Lanham Act. 15 U.S. Code § 1114 states that any unauthorized 'use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark' that is 'likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive' constitutes infringement. By extending the period from twelve to eighteen months, SpinCo increases the likelihood of consumer confusion, especially if the trademark is still in use in the same manner as before. This extended use could therefore violate the Lanham Act by infringing on the Parent's trademark rights without explicit consent, as this could lead to a likelihood of confusion as prohibited by 15 U.S.C. § 1114.

perturbation.original_text: The Party providing Information pursuant to this Section 5.1(b) shall only be obligated to provide such Information in the form, condition and format in which it then exists, and in no event shall such Party be required to perform any improvement, modification, conversion, updating or reformatting of any such Information, and nothing in this Section 5.1 shall expand the obligations of a Party under Section 5.4.
perturbation.changed_text: The Party providing Information pursuant to this Section 5.1(b) shall only be obligated to provide such Information in a non-readable format. In no event shall such Party be required to perform any improvement, modification, conversion, updating or reformatting of any such Information, and nothing in this Section 5.1 shall expand the obligations of a Party under Section 5.4.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the providing party to deliver information in a non-readable format could render the information useless and obstruct the purpose of the information exchange clause. This could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if the information is essential for the other party to fulfill its obligations or exercise its rights under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While the first link is not perfect and relates to slavery which may be confusing in this context, the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 is a well-established standard in the US. The first link is a backup. By mandating a "non-readable format", the agreement undermines the implied duty of good faith, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in fulfilling their contractual obligations. Delivering information in an intentionally unusable state is a violation of the spirit of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates the provision of information in a 'non-readable format,' which directly undermines the purpose of information exchange. While the provided scraped snippets are not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation clearly state that this change violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably. Providing information in a non-readable format obstructs the recipient's ability to use the information, thereby contradicting the implied legal obligation to facilitate the contract's objectives, thus creating a legal contradiction.
